The `queueMessageForBusySession` stores:\n- `platform: p`\n- `replyCtx: msg.ReplyCtx`\n- `content: msg.Content` (which could be empty for image-only messages)\n- `images: msg.Images`\n- `files: msg.Files`\n- etc.\n\nBut in `processInteractiveEvents`, when dequeuing a message, it does:\n```go\ngo func() {\n    nextSend <- state.agentSession.Send(queuedPrompt, queued.images, queued.files)\n}()\n```\n\nSo images ARE passed through. That should work.\n\nHmm, let me re-examine the race condition more carefully. The comment says:\n\n```go\n// Guard against a narrow race: a message may have been queued between\n// processInteractiveEvents observing an empty queue and returning here\n// (session is still locked, so handleMessage\'s TryLock fails and routes\n// the message to queueMessageForBusySession). Drain any such orphans.\nif e.drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey) {\n    unlocked = true\n}\n```\n\nBut wait - `processInteractiveEvents` at lines 2773-2848 DOES process queued messages within the loop. So after it returns, the queue should be empty (unless there\'s a race). Then `drainPendingMessages` handles the race.\n\nBut there\'s another subtle issue. Let me look at `drainPendingMessages` again:\n\n```go\nfunc (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(state *interactiveState, session *Session, sessions *SessionManager, sessionKey string) bool {\n    for {\n        state.mu.Lock()\n        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {\n            session.Unlock()\n            state.mu.Unlock()\n            return true\n        }\n        queued := state.pendingMessages[0]\n        state.pendingMessages = state.pendingMessages[1:]\n        ...\n        state.mu.Unlock()\n\n        e.i18n.DetectAndSet(queued.content)\n        prompt := e.buildSenderPrompt(queued.content, queued.userID, queued.msgPlatform, queued.msgSessionKey)\n\n        if state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {\n            ...\n        }\n\n        drainEvents(state.agentSession.Events())\n\n        session.AddHistory("user", queued.content)\n\n        sendDone := make(chan error, 1)\n        go func() {\n            sendDone <- state.agentSession.Send(prompt, queued.images, queued.files)\n        }()\n\n        ...\n        slog.Info("processing queued message", "session", sessionKey)\n        e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, sessionKey, "", time.Now(), stopTyping, sendDone, queued.replyCtx)\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThis processes queued messages one at a time, calling `processInteractiveEvents` for each. After `processInteractiveEvents` returns, the loop goes back to check if there are more queued messages.\n\nBut wait - `processInteractiveEvents` has its own internal drain too! Let me trace through what happens:\n\n1. `processInteractiveMessageWith` is called for message 1\n2. `processInteractiveEvents` runs, gets EventResult for message 1\n3. At line 2776, checks `len(state.pendingMessages) > 0` - if yes, dequeues one and continues the loop\n4. The loop processes message 2 (which was queued)\n5. When message 2\'s EventResult comes, checks again for more queued messages\n6. Processes message 3, etc.\n7. When no more queued messages, returns\n\nThen back in `processInteractiveMessageWith`:\n8. `drainPendingMessages` is called - this should find empty queue and unlock\n\nThis all seems correct. So why are messages being dropped?\n\nLet me look at the `drainPendingMessages` more carefully. There\'s something odd:\n\n```go\nfunc (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(...) bool {\n    for {\n        state.mu.Lock()\n        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {\n            session.Unlock()\n            state.mu.Unlock()\n            return true\n        }\n        ...\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThis unlocks the session when the queue is empty. But what if `processInteractiveEvents` has already drained all queued messages from within the loop? Then `drainPendingMessages` would find an empty queue, unlock, and return.\n\nBut wait - there\'s a potential problem. `processInteractiveEvents` at line 2776 processes queued messages, but it doesn\'t call `session.Unlock()` between processing them. The session stays locked the whole time. So when `processInteractiveEvents` returns, the session is still locked. Then `drainPendingMessages` is called, which finds the queue empty and unlocks.\n\nThat seems fine.\n\nLet me think about this differently. Let me look at when the issue could happen. The PR says this is with feishu (WebSocket mode). Maybe there\'s something specific about how feishu delivers messages.\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read the PR more carefully:\n\n> When user sends multiple messages rapidly, cc-connect only processes the first one, and the rest are silently dropped during session locking. After session unlocks, they\'re not processed either.\n\nMaybe the issue is that the queue IS working but the response messages (MsgMessageQueued) aren\'t being delivered, making it look like messages are dropped? Or maybe the queue ISN\'T working because of a state issue?\n\nLet me look at how the interactive state is cleaned up. The `cleanupInteractiveState` function deletes the interactive state. When does this happen?\n\nLooking at when cleanup is called:\n1. In `processInteractiveEvents` at the `channelClosed` label (line 2879)\n2. In `processInteractiveEvents` at `EventError` with dead agent (line 2869)\n3. In `processInteractiveEvents` at idle timeout (line 2403)\n4. Via `cleanupInteractiveState` from outside\n\nAfter a normal turn completes (EventResult), cleanup is NOT called. The state persists. The agent session stays alive. So the next message should be able to queue.\n\nBut wait, there\'s something else. Let me look at `processInteractiveEvents` more carefully. When EventResult is received and there are no queued messages, it returns. But the `drainPendingMessages` is called AFTER `processInteractiveEvents` returns. But what if `processInteractiveEvents` ALREADY drained the queue (processing queued messages in its own loop) and then returns?\n\nLet me trace through the exact sequence:\n\n1. Message 1 is processed. `processInteractiveEvents` is called.\n2. While processing message 1, messages 2 and 3 arrive and are queued via `queueMessageForBusySession`.\n3. EventResult for message 1 arrives.\n4. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 2 entries (messages 2 and 3). It dequeues message 2.\n5. The loop continues (because of `continue` at line 2847).\n6. Message 2\'s turn runs. While it\'s running, no new messages arrive (hypothetically).\n7. EventResult for message 2 arrives.\n8. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 1 entry (message 3). It dequeues message 3.\n9. The loop continues.\n10. Message 3\'s turn runs.\n11. EventResult for message 3 arrives.\n12. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 0 entries. Goes to line 2849, unlocks, returns.\n13. Back in `processInteractiveMessageWith`, `drainPendingMessages` is called at line 2013.\n14. `drainPendingMessages` finds empty queue, unlocks (again? The lock was already released at step 12!), and returns.\n\nWait, step 12 says `state.mu.Unlock()` at line 2849. But what about `session.Unlock()`? Let me check...\n\nAt line 2849:\n```go\nstate.mu.Unlock()\n```\n\nThis only unlocks the state mutex, NOT the session lock! The session lock is held by `processInteractiveMessageWith` (from line 1472 or 1562). OK so the messages are: 
- `MsgMessageQueued`: "📬 Message received — will process after the current task finishes."
- `MsgPreviousProcessing`: "⏳ Previous request still processing. Use `/btw <message>` to add context to the current turn."

So if queuing works, the user should see "📬 Message received" for the 2nd and 3rd messages. If it doesn't work, they'd see "⏳ Previous request still processing".

But the PR says messages are "silently dropped". Let me think about this differently. Maybe the issue is that messages with images (like the PR describes) have `len(msg.Images) > 0`, and this somehow bypasses the queue?

Let me look at the flow again for images. In `handleMessage`:

```go
content := strings.TrimSpace(msg.Content)
if content == "" && len(msg.Images) == 0 && len(msg.Files) == 0 && msg.Location == nil {
    return
}
```

Then later:
```go
if len(msg.Images) == 0 && strings.HasPrefix(content, "/") {
    if e.handleCommand(p, msg, content) {
        return
    }
}
```

So for messages with only images (no text content), they pass through. But then, does the queue work? Let me check...

Wait, I think I found the issue! Let me look at what happens with the queue more carefully. The `queueMessageForBusySession` stores:
- `platform: p`
- `replyCtx: msg.ReplyCtx`
- `content: msg.Content` (which could be empty for image-only messages)
- `images: msg.Images`
- `files: msg.Files`
- etc.

But in `processInteractiveEvents`, when dequeuing a message, it does:
```go
go func() {
    nextSend <- state.agentSession.Send(queuedPrompt, queued.images, queued.files)
}()
```

So images ARE passed through. That should work.

Hmm, let me re-examine the race condition more carefully. The comment says:

```go
// Guard against a narrow race: a message may have been queued between
// processInteractiveEvents observing an empty queue and returning here
// (session is still locked, so handleMessage's TryLock fails and routes
// the message to queueMessageForBusySession). Drain any such orphans.
if e.drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey) {
    unlocked = true
}
```

But wait - `processInteractiveEvents` at lines 2773-2848 DOES process queued messages within the loop. So after it returns, the queue should be empty (unless there's a race). Then `drainPendingMessages` handles the race.

But there's another subtle issue. Let me look at `drainPendingMessages` again:

```go
func (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(state *interactiveState, session *Session, sessions *SessionManager, sessionKey string) bool {
    for {
        state.mu.Lock()
        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {
            session.Unlock()
            state.mu.Unlock()
            return true
        }
        queued := state.pendingMessages[0]
        state.pendingMessages = state.pendingMessages[1:]
        ...
        state.mu.Unlock()

        e.i18n.DetectAndSet(queued.content)
        prompt := e.buildSenderPrompt(queued.content, queued.userID, queued.msgPlatform, queued.msgSessionKey)

        if state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {
            ...
        }

        drainEvents(state.agentSession.Events())

        session.AddHistory("user", queued.content)

        sendDone := make(chan error, 1)
        go func() {
            sendDone <- state.agentSession.Send(prompt, queued.images, queued.files)
        }()

        ...
        slog.Info("processing queued message", "session", sessionKey)
        e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, sessionKey, "", time.Now(), stopTyping, sendDone, queued.replyCtx)
    }
}
```

This processes queued messages one at a time, calling `processInteractiveEvents` for each. After `processInteractiveEvents` returns, the loop goes back to check if there are more queued messages.

But wait - `processInteractiveEvents` has its own internal drain too! Let me trace through what happens:

1. `processInteractiveMessageWith` is called for message 1
2. `processInteractiveEvents` runs, gets EventResult for message 1
3. At line 2776, checks `len(state.pendingMessages) > 0` - if yes, dequeues one and continues the loop
4. The loop processes message 2 (which was queued)
5. When message 2's EventResult comes, checks again for more queued messages
6. Processes message 3, etc.
7. When no more queued messages, returns

Then back in `processInteractiveMessageWith`:
8. `drainPendingMessages` is called - this should find empty queue and unlock

This all seems correct. So why are messages being dropped?

Let me look at the `drainPendingMessages` more carefully. There's something odd:

```go
func (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(...) bool {
    for {
        state.mu.Lock()
        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {
            session.Unlock()
            state.mu.Unlock()
            return true
        }
        ...
    }
}
```

This unlocks the session when the queue is empty. But what if `processInteractiveEvents` has already drained all queued messages from within the loop? Then `drainPendingMessages` would find an empty queue, unlock, and return.

But wait - there's a potential problem. `processInteractiveEvents` at line 2776 processes queued messages, but it doesn't call `session.Unlock()` between processing them. The session stays locked the whole time. So when `processInteractiveEvents` returns, the session is still locked. Then `drainPendingMessages` is called, which finds the queue empty and unlocks.

That seems fine.

Let me think about this differently. Let me look at when the issue could happen. The PR says this is with feishu (WebSocket mode). Maybe there's something specific about how feishu delivers messages.

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the PR more carefully:

> When user sends multiple messages rapidly, cc-connect only processes the first one, and the rest are silently dropped during session locking. After session unlocks, they're not processed either.

Maybe the issue is that the queue IS working but the response messages (MsgMessageQueued) aren't being delivered, making it look like messages are dropped? Or maybe the queue ISN'T working because of a state issue?

Let me look at how the interactive state is cleaned up. The `cleanupInteractiveState` function deletes the interactive state. When does this happen?

Looking at when cleanup is called:
1. In `processInteractiveEvents` at the `channelClosed` label (line 2879)
2. In `processInteractiveEvents` at `EventError` with dead agent (line 2869)
3. In `processInteractiveEvents` at idle timeout (line 2403)
4. Via `cleanupInteractiveState` from outside

After a normal turn completes (EventResult), cleanup is NOT called. The state persists. The agent session stays alive. So the next message should be able to queue.

But wait, there's something else. Let me look at `processInteractiveEvents` more carefully. When EventResult is received and there are no queued messages, it returns. But the `drainPendingMessages` is called AFTER `processInteractiveEvents` returns. But what if `processInteractiveEvents` ALREADY drained the queue (processing queued messages in its own loop) and then returns?

Let me trace through the exact sequence:

1. Message 1 is processed. `processInteractiveEvents` is called.
2. While processing message 1, messages 2 and 3 arrive and are queued via `queueMessageForBusySession`.
3. EventResult for message 1 arrives.
4. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 2 entries (messages 2 and 3). It dequeues message 2.
5. The loop continues (because of `continue` at line 2847).
6. Message 2's turn runs. While it's running, no new messages arrive (hypothetically).
7. EventResult for message 2 arrives.
8. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 1 entry (message 3). It dequeues message 3.
9. The loop continues.
10. Message 3's turn runs.
11. EventResult for message 3 arrives.
12. Line 2776: `state.pendingMessages` has 0 entries. Goes to line 2849, unlocks, returns.
13. Back in `processInteractiveMessageWith`, `drainPendingMessages` is called at line 2013.
14. `drainPendingMessages` finds empty queue, unlocks (again? The lock was already released at step 12!), and returns.

Wait, step 12 says `state.mu.Unlock()` at line 2849. But what about `session.Unlock()`? Let me check...

At line 2849:
```go
state.mu.Unlock()
```

This only unlocks the state mutex, NOT the session lock! The session lock is held by `processInteractiveMessageWith` (from line 1472 or 1562). The session is never unlocked within `processInteractiveEvents`!

So the sequence is:
- `processInteractiveMessageWith` locks the session (via TryLock in handleMessage)
- `processInteractiveEvents` runs, processes all events including queued messages
- `processInteractiveEvents` returns
- `drainPendingMessages` is called
- `drainPendingMessages` finds empty queue, calls `session.Unlock()`, returns

This is correct. The session stays locked throughout all queued message processing and is only unlocked when the queue is truly empty.

Now, what if there's a bug in the race guard? Let me look at handleMessage again:

```go
if !session.TryLock() {
    ...
    if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
        // Race guard: ...
        if session.TryLock() {
            go e.drainOrphanedQueue(session, sessions, interactiveKey, agent, resolvedWorkspace)
        }
        return
    }
    e.reply(p, msg.ReplyCtx, e.i18n.T(MsgPreviousProcessing))
    return
}
```

The race guard: after queueing, it tries to lock the session again. If successful, it means the main processor already finished and unlocked. In that case, it calls `drainOrphanedQueue` which calls `drainPendingMessages`.

But wait, what if `drainPendingMessages` in the main processor finds the queue and processes it? Let's trace:

1. Main processor finishes its last turn, calls `drainPendingMessages`
2. At line 2933: `state.mu.Lock()`
3. At line 2934: checks `len(state.pendingMessages) == 0` → it's NOT empty (race message just queued)
4. Dequeues the message
5. Calls `state.mu.Unlock()` at line 2944
6. Processes the message

But between steps 3 and 4, what if the racing handler's `queueMessageForBusySession` hasn't finished yet? It's holding `state.mu` (via `state.mu.Lock()` at line 1589 of `queueMessageForBusySession`). So the main processor's `drainPendingMessages` would block at step 2 until the queue is done.

Then, after `drainPendingMessages` dequeues and processes the message, it returns. Then the racing handler's race guard tries to lock the session: `session.TryLock()` returns false (because `drainPendingMessages` didn't unlock - it's still processing). So the racing handler returns without starting a duplicate processor.

That seems correct.

But wait, there's another scenario. What if `drainPendingMessages` finishes, unlocks the session, and THEN the racing handler's `session.TryLock()` succeeds? That would start a duplicate `drainOrphanedQueue`. But `drainOrphanedQueue` would find the queue empty and just unlock. So it's harmless.

OK so the logic seems correct. Let me now think about what ELSE could go wrong.

Actually, wait. Let me look at the `processInteractiveEvents` drain more carefully:

```go
// Line 2773-2849
// Check for queued messages — if present, continue the event loop
// for the next turn instead of returning.
state.mu.Lock()
if len(state.pendingMessages) > 0 {
    queued := state.pendingMessages[0]
    state.pendingMessages = state.pendingMessages[1:]
    remainingQueue := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.platform = queued.platform
    state.replyCtx = queued.replyCtx
    state.fromVoice = queued.fromVoice
    state.mu.Unlock()
    ...
    continue
}
state.mu.Unlock()
```

This processes ONE queued message per EventResult. So after processing all queued messages, when there are no more, it falls through to `state.mu.Unlock()` and then returns.

But what if the processing of a queued message results in an `EventError`? Let's look at that:

```go
case EventError:
    cp.Finalize(ProgressCardStateFailed)
    sp.discard()
    if event.Error != nil {
        slog.Error("agent error", "error", event.Error)
        e.send(p, replyCtx, fmt.Sprintf(e.i18n.T(MsgError), event.Error))
    }
    // Only drop queued messages if the agent session is dead.
    if state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {
        e.notifyDroppedQueuedMessages(state, event.Error)
    }
    return
```

Ah! If a queued message's turn results in an `EventError` and the agent is still alive, the function just RETURNS without draining remaining queued messages! The remaining queued messages are left in the queue!

But then `processInteractiveMessageWith` calls `drainPendingMessages` at line 2013. Let's check what `drainPendingMessages` does:

```go
func (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(...) bool {
    for {
        state.mu.Lock()
        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {
            session.Unlock()
            state.mu.Unlock()
            return true
        }
        ...
    }
}
```

This would find the remaining messages and process them. So even if `processInteractiveEvents` returns early due to `EventError`, `drainPendingMessages` would still process remaining queued messages.

But what about the `channelClosed` case? In that case, `processInteractiveEvents` calls `notifyDroppedQueuedMessages` which drains and notifies. So no issue there.

Hmm, I'm struggling to find the bug. Let me look at the test files for any hints about what the expected behavior is and what might be broken.

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /testbed/core/engine.go:
  1617	// drainOrphanedQueue is called when a message was queued but the drain loop
  1618	// has already exited. It processes all pending messages in the state, similar
  1619	// to the drain loop in processInteractiveMessageWith but as a standalone
  1620	// goroutine.
  1621	func (e *Engine) drainOrphanedQueue(session *Session, sessions *SessionManager, interactiveKey string, agent Agent, workspaceDir string) {
  1622	        unlocked := false
  1623	        defer func() {
  1624	                if !unlocked {
  1625	                        session.Unlock()
  1626	                }
  1627	        }()
  1628	
  1629	        e.interactiveMu.Lock()
  1630	        state, hasState := e.interactiveStates[interactiveKey]
  1631	        e.interactiveMu.Unlock()
  1632	
  1633	        if !hasState || state == nil || state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {
  1634	                if hasState && state != nil {
  1635	                        e.notifyDroppedQueuedMessages(state, fmt.Errorf("agent session ended"))
  1636	                }
  1637	                return
  1638	        }
  1639	
  1640	        unlocked = e.drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey)
  1641	}
  1642

2026-05-29 18:04:29,106 - INFO - swea-agent-chenhg5__cc-connect-567 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now let me look more carefully at the main race guard code in handleMessage:

```go
if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
    // Race guard: the drain loop in processInteractiveMessageWith may
    // have just finished (session unlocked) between our TryLock failure
    // and the queue append. Re-try TryLock — if it succeeds, no one is
    // draining the queue so we must start a processor ourselves.
    if session.TryLock() {
        go e.drainOrphanedQueue(session, sessions, interactiveKey, agent, resolvedWorkspace)
    }
    return
}
```

Wait! I think I found a potential issue. Look at this sequence:

1. `handleMessage` is called for message 1. `session.TryLock()` succeeds.
2. `processInteractiveMessageWith` is started in a goroutine.
3. `processInteractiveMessageWith` calls `getOrCreateInteractiveStateWith` which creates the state and agent session.
4. `processInteractiveEvents` starts running.
5. Meanwhile, message 2 arrives. `handleMessage` is called. `session.TryLock()` fails (session is busy).
6. `queueMessageForBusySession` is called. It finds the state, queues message 2. Returns true.
7. Race guard: `session.TryLock()` fails (still busy). Returns.
8. Message 2 is queued.

Now, in the main event loop:
9. EventResult for message 1 arrives.
10. The loop checks for queued messages at line 2776.
11. It dequeues message 2, processes it.

This works fine. But what about a different timing:

1. Message 1's EventResult arrives.
2. The loop checks `pendingMessages` → empty (not yet queued).
3. Falls through to `return`.
4. `processInteractiveMessageWith` calls `drainPendingMessages`.
5. `drainPendingMessages` locks `state.mu`, checks `len(state.pendingMessages)`.
6. Meanwhile, `handleMessage` for message 2: `queueMessageForBusySession` locks `state.mu` and appends.
7. `drainPendingMessages` finds the message, dequeues it, processes it.

This also works. The mutex ensures correct ordering.

But what about this scenario:
1. `drainPendingMessages` finds queue empty, unlocks session.
2. Just after unlocking, `handleMessage` for message 2: `session.TryLock()` fails... no wait, the session was just unlocked so TryLock should succeed. Let me re-trace.

Actually, wait. The race guard in `handleMessage` is:

```go
if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
    if session.TryLock() {
        go e.drainOrphanedQueue(...)
    }
    return
}
```

If `drainPendingMessages` has already unlocked the session, then `session.TryLock()` would succeed, and `drainOrphanedQueue` would process the message. That's correct.

But what if the timing is:
1. `drainPendingMessages` is about to check the queue.
2. `handleMessage` for message 2: `queueMessageForBusySession` queues the message.
3. `drainPendingMessages` finds the message, processes it.
4. `handleMessage` race guard: `session.TryLock()` - but `drainPendingMessages` hasn't unlocked yet (it's still processing). TryLock fails.
5. The message is being processed by `drainPendingMessages`. Good.
6. Eventually `drainPendingMessages` finishes and unlocks.

But then there's another scenario:
1. `processInteractiveEvents` returns (EventResult, no queued messages).
2. Before `drainPendingMessages` is called, `handleMessage` for message 2 queues the message.
3. `drainPendingMessages` finds the message and processes it.
4. All good.

And yet another:
1. `processInteractiveEvents` returns.
2. `drainPendingMessages` runs, finds queue empty, unlocks.
3. After unlock, message 2 arrives. `handleMessage` → `TryLock` succeeds → processes normally in new goroutine.

All scenarios seem covered. So why are messages being dropped?

Let me look at this from another angle. Maybe the issue is with the feishu platform specifically. Let me check how messages are received in the feishu platform.

   322	// startWebSocketMode starts the WebSocket long connection mode (for Feishu domestic version)
   323	func (p *Platform) startWebSocketMode() error {
   324	        wsOpts := []larkws.ClientOption{
   325	                larkws.WithEventHandler(p.eventHandler),
   326	                larkws.WithLogLevel(larkcore.LogLevelInfo),
   327	                larkws.WithLogger(&sanitizingLogger{inner: larkcore.NewEventLogger()}),
   328	        }
   329	        if p.domain != lark.FeishuBaseUrl {
   330	                wsOpts = append(wsOpts, larkws.WithDomain(p.domain))
   331	        }
   332	        p.wsClient = larkws.NewClient(p.appID, p.appSecret, wsOpts...)
   333	
   334	        ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
   335	        p.cancel = cancel
   336	
   337	        go func() {
   338	                if err := p.wsClient.Start(ctx); err != nil {
   339	                        slog.Error(p.tag()+": websocket error", "error", err)
   340	                }
   341	        }()
   342	
   343	        return nil
   344	}

   644	func (p *Platform) onMessage(ctx context.Context, event *larkim.P2MessageReceiveV1) error {
   645	        msg := event.Event.Message
   646	        sender := event.Event.Sender
   647	
   648	        msgType := ""
   649	        if msg.MessageType != nil {
   650	                msgType = *msg.MessageType
   651	        }
   652	
   653	        chatID := ""
   654	        if msg.ChatId != nil {
   655	                chatID = *msg.ChatId
   656	        }
   657	        userID := ""
   658	        userName := ""
   659	        if sender.SenderId != nil && sender.SenderId.OpenId != nil {
   660	                userID = *sender.SenderId.OpenId
   661	        }
   662	        if userID != "" {
   663	                userName = p.resolveUserName(userID)
   664	        }
   665	        chatName := p.resolveChatName(chatID)
   666	
   667	        messageID := ""
   668	        if msg.MessageId != nil {
   669	                messageID = *msg.MessageId
   670	        }
   671	
   672	        if p.dedup.IsDuplicate(messageID) {
   673	                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": duplicate message ignored", "message_id", messageID)
   674	                return nil
   675	        }
   676	
   677	        if msg.CreateTime != nil {
   678	                if ms, err := strconv.ParseInt(*msg.CreateTime, 10, 64); err == nil {
   679	                        msgTime := time.Unix(ms/1000, (ms%1000)*int64(time.Millisecond))
   680	                        if core.IsOldMessage(msgTime) {
   681	                                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": ignoring old message after restart", "create_time", *msg.CreateTime)
   682	                                return nil
   683	                        }
   684	                }
   685	        }
   686	
   687	        chatType := ""
   688	        if msg.ChatType != nil {
   689	                chatType = *msg.ChatType
   690	        }
   691	        mentionCount := len(msg.Mentions)
   692	        slog.Debug(p.tag()+": inbound message",
   693	                "message_id", messageID,
   694	                "chat_id", chatID,
   695	                "chat_type", chatType,
   696	                "root_id", stringValue(msg.RootId),
   697	                "thread_id", stringValue(msg.ThreadId),
   698	                "parent_id", stringValue(msg.ParentId),
   699	                "mentions", mentionCount,
   700	                "group_reply_all", p.groupReplyAll,
   701	                "thread_isolation", p.threadIsolation,
   702	        )
   703	
   704	        if chatType == "group" && !p.groupReplyAll && p.botOpenID != "" {
   705	                if !isBotMentioned(msg.Mentions, p.botOpenID) {
   706	                        // Feishu @all sends {"text":"@_all"} with 0 mentions.
   707	                        if p.respondToAtEveryoneAndHere && msg.Content != nil && strings.Contains(*msg.Content, "@_all") {
   708	                                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": responding to @all message", "chat_id", chatID)
   709	                        } else {
   710	                                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": ignoring group message without bot mention", "chat_id", chatID)
   711	                                return nil
   712	                        }
   713	                }
   714	        }
   715	
   716	        if !core.AllowList(p.allowFrom, userID) {
   717	                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": message from unauthorized user", "user", userID)
   718	                return nil
   719	        }
   720	
   721	        if msg.Content == nil && msgType != "merge_forward" {
   722	                slog.Debug(p.tag()+": message content is nil", "message_id", messageID, "type", msgType)
   723	                return nil
   724	        }
   725	
   726	        // Capture content before going async — the SDK may reuse the event object.
   727	        content := ""
   728	        if msg.Content != nil {
   729	                content = *msg.Content
   730	        }
   731	        mentions := msg.Mentions
   732	        parentID := stringValue(msg.ParentId)
   733	
   734	        sessionKey := p.makeSessionKey(msg, chatID, userID)
   735	        rctx := replyContext{messageID: messageID, chatID: chatID, sessionKey: sessionKey}
   736	        slog.Debug(p.tag()+": routed inbound message",
   737	                "message_id", messageID,
   738	                "session_key", sessionKey,
   739	                "reply_in_thread", p.shouldReplyInThread(rctx),
   740	        )
   741	
   742	        // Dispatch message handling asynchronously so the SDK event loop is not
   743	        // blocked by IO-heavy operations (image/audio download, handler HTTP calls).
   744	        // The dedup and old-message checks above remain synchronous to guarantee
   745	        // correctness before spawning the goroutine.
   746	        go p.dispatchMessage(ctx, msgType, content, mentions, messageID, sessionKey, userID, userName, chatName, rctx, parentID)
   747	
   748	        return nil
   749	}
   750	
   751	// dispatchMessage handles the message content parsing, media download, and
   752	// handler invocation. It runs in its own goroutine so that onMessage returns
   753	// quickly and does not block the SDK event loop.
   754	func (p *Platform) dispatchMessage(ctx context.Context, msgType, content string, mentions []*larkim.MentionEvent, messageID, sessionKey, userID, userName, chatName string, rctx replyContext, parentID string) {
   755	        // If this message is a reply to another message, fetch the quoted content
   756	        // and prepend it so the agent has full context.
   757	        quotedPrefix := ""
   758	        if parentID != "" {
   759	                quotedPrefix = p.fetchQuotedMessage(ctx, parentID)
   760	        }
   761	
   762	        switch msgType {
   763	        case "text":
   764	                var textBody struct {
   765	                        Text string `json:"text"`
   766	                }
   767	                if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(content), &textBody); err != nil {
   768	                        slog.Error(p.tag()+": failed to parse text content", "error", err)
   769	                        return
   770	                }
   771	                text := stripMentions(textBody.Text, mentions, p.botOpenID)
   772	                if text == "" {
   773	                        slog.Debug(p.tag()+": dropping empty text after mention stripping",
   774	                                "message_id", messageID,
   775	                                "raw_text_len", len(textBody.Text),
   776	                                "mentions", len(mentions),
   777	                        )
   778	                        return
   779	                }
   780	                p.handler(p.dispatchPlatform(), &core.Message{
   781	                        SessionKey: sessionKey, Platform: p.platformName,
   782	                        MessageID: messageID,
   783	                        UserID:    userID, UserName: userName, ChatName: chatName,
   784	                        Content: quotedPrefix + text, ReplyCtx: rctx,
   785	                })
   786	
   787	        case "image":
   788	                var imgBody struct {
   789	                        ImageKey string `json:"image_key"`
   790	                }
   791	                if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(content), &imgBody); err != nil {
   792	                        slog.Error(p.tag()+": failed to parse image content", "error", err)
   793	                        return
   794	                }
   795	                imgData, mimeType, err := p.downloadImage(messageID, imgBody.ImageKey)
   796	                if err != nil {
   797	                        slog.Error(p.tag()+": download image failed", "error", err)
   798	                        return
   799	                }
   800	                p.handler(p.dispatchPlatform(), &core.Message{

OK so I see: for each message, `dispatchMessage` runs in its own goroutine (line 746). So multiple messages can be processed concurrently in terms of downloading images, etc. Then each calls `p.handler(...)` which goes to `engine.HandleMessage`.

But wait, there's an important detail: `dispatchMessage` does the download (which takes time), THEN calls `p.handler`. So the sequence is:

1. Message 1: goroutine starts, downloads image, calls handler. Handler locks session, starts processing.
2. Message 2: goroutine starts, downloads image, calls handler. Handler tries to lock session, fails, queues.
3. Message 3: goroutine starts, downloads image, calls handler. Handler tries to lock session, fails, queues.

This all seems fine.

Now let me think about what could cause the queue to not work. Let me look at whether the issue might be with the `interactiveKey`. In `handleMessage`:

```go
interactiveKey := msg.SessionKey
if e.multiWorkspace && wsSessions != nil {
    sessions = wsSessions
    agent = wsAgent
    interactiveKey = resolvedWorkspace + ":" + msg.SessionKey
}
```

```go
func (e *Engine) queueMessageForBusySession(p Platform, msg *Message, interactiveKey string) bool {
    e.interactiveMu.Lock()
    state, hasState := e.interactiveStates[interactiveKey]
    ...
}
```

What if there's a mismatch between the `interactiveKey` used to create the state and the one used to look it up? Let's check.

In `processInteractiveMessageWith`:
```go
state := e.getOrCreateInteractiveStateWith(interactiveKey, p, msg.ReplyCtx, session, sessions, agentOverride, ccSessionKey)
```

This stores the state with the same `interactiveKey`. So there shouldn't be a mismatch.

Wait, actually I just realized something. The `drainOrphanedQueue` receives a `sessions` and `agent` parameter that could differ from what was used to create the state. Let me look at the call site:

```go
// In handleMessage (line 1494):
if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
    if session.TryLock() {
        go e.drainOrphanedQueue(session, sessions, interactiveKey, agent, resolvedWorkspace)
    }
    return
}
```

This passes `sessions` and `agent` which may be workspace-specific. But `drainOrphanedQueue` passes them to `drainPendingMessages`:

```go
unlocked = e.drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey)
```

But wait, `drainPendingMessages` doesn't use the `sessions` parameter for anything other than passing it to `processInteractiveEvents`:

```go

```

And `processInteractiveEvents` uses `sessions` only for `sessions.Save()` at line 2668. So that's fine.

Hmm, let me look at this from yet another angle. Let me see if the problem might be in `processInteractiveMessageWith` itself. Let me look at lines 2009-2015 again:

```go
// Guard against a narrow race: a message may have been queued between
// processInteractiveEvents observing an empty queue and returning here
// (session is still locked, so handleMessage's TryLock fails and routes
// the message to queueMessageForBusySession). Drain any such orphans.
if e.drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey) {
    unlocked = true
}
```

What if `drainPendingMessages` is NOT being called because `processInteractiveEvents` returned via some other path that doesn't reach this code? Let me check...

`processInteractiveEvents` can return via:
1. `stopCh` (line 2368-2369)
2. `channelClosed` (line 2372 - goto)
3. `pendingSend` error (line 2374-2393)
4. `idleCh` timeout (line 2394-2404)
5. `e.ctx.Done()` (line 2405-2406)
6. `state.isStopped()` (line 2409-2412)
7. Normal return after EventResult (line 2856)
8. EventError return (line 2871)

In cases 1-6, `drainPendingMessages` is still called (since they all return to the caller which calls `drainPendingMessages`). But wait, for case 1 (stopCh), if the state was stopped mid-processing, any queued messages would be in the queue. But `drainPendingMessages` would find them and process them. However, if the agent session is dead at that point, `drainPendingMessages` would just notify and not process them. That's probably fine.

Actually, wait. Let me look at case 7 more carefully. After `processInteractiveEvents` returns normally (EventResult with no queued messages), `processInteractiveMessageWith` continues to line 2009 and calls `drainPendingMessages`. This should work.

But what about the `drainPendingMessages` function itself? Let me look at it again:

```go
func (e *Engine) drainPendingMessages(state *interactiveState, session *Session, sessions *SessionManager, sessionKey string) bool {
    for {
        state.mu.Lock()
        if len(state.pendingMessages) == 0 {
            session.Unlock()
            state.mu.Unlock()
            return true
        }
        queued := state.pendingMessages[0]
        state.pendingMessages = state.pendingMessages[1:]
        state.platform = queued.platform
        state.replyCtx = queued.replyCtx
        state.fromVoice = queued.fromVoice
        state.mu.Unlock()

        e.i18n.DetectAndSet(queued.content)
        prompt := e.buildSenderPrompt(queued.content, queued.userID, queued.msgPlatform, queued.msgSessionKey)

        if state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {
            e.send(queued.platform, queued.replyCtx, fmt.Sprintf(e.i18n.T(MsgError), "agent session ended"))
            e.notifyDroppedQueuedMessages(state, fmt.Errorf("agent session ended"))
            return false
        }

        drainEvents(state.agentSession.Events())

        session.AddHistory("user", queued.content)

        sendDone := make(chan error, 1)
        go func() {
            sendDone <- state.agentSession.Send(prompt, queued.images, queued.files)
        }()

        var stopTyping func()
        if ti, ok := queued.platform.(TypingIndicator); ok {
            stopTyping = ti.StartTyping(e.ctx, queued.replyCtx)
        }

        slog.Info("processing queued message", "session", sessionKey)
        e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, sessionKey, "", time.Now(), stopTyping, sendDone, queued.replyCtx)
    }
}
```

I notice this: after `processInteractiveEvents` returns (for the queued message), the loop goes back to check for more queued messages. But what if `processInteractiveEvents` returned because of an error (like channel closed)? Then it wouldn't continue to process remaining queued messages. BUT the loop would continue because it's a `for` loop that only breaks when `len(state.pendingMessages) == 0`.

Wait, but what if `processInteractiveEvents` calls `notifyDroppedQueuedMessages`? Let's check: `processInteractiveEvents` at `channelClosed` calls `e.notifyDroppedQueuedMessages(state, ...)`. This drains ALL pending messages and sets `state.pendingMessages = nil`. So when the loop comes back, `len(state.pendingMessages) == 0` and it returns.

Similarly, at `EventError` with dead agent, it calls `e.notifyDroppedQueuedMessages` which also drains. So in those cases, the loop terminates.

But for `EventError` with alive agent, it does NOT drain. It returns. Then `drainPendingMessages` would find remaining messages and process them. Good.

Hmm, I'm really struggling to find the bug. Let me try a different approach - let me look at the code changes between versions or look for recent commits related to this issue.

Actually, let me reconsider the problem statement. The PR says "session 解锁后也不会补处理". Maybe the issue is simpler than I think. Let me look at whether `drainPendingMessages` is actually being called at all after a normal turn.

Wait, I should look more carefully at the flow in `processInteractiveEvents`. Specifically at lines 2773-2849:

```go
// Check for queued messages — if present, continue the event loop
// for the next turn instead of returning.
state.mu.Lock()
if len(state.pendingMessages) > 0 {
    queued := state.pendingMessages[0]
    state.pendingMessages = state.pendingMessages[1:]
    remainingQueue := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.platform = queued.platform
    state.replyCtx = queued.replyCtx
    state.fromVoice = queued.fromVoice
    state.mu.Unlock()
    ...
    // Reset per-turn state
    textParts = nil
    segmentStart = 0
    toolCount = 0
    turnStart = time.Now()
    ...
    session.AddHistory("user", queued.content)
    ...
    slog.Info("processing queued message",
        "session", sessionKey,
        "remaining_queue", remainingQueue,
    )
    continue
}
state.mu.Unlock()
```

The `continue` at line 2847 goes back to the top of the `for` loop at line 2362, which re-enters the `select` to wait for the next event. So it processes one queued message at a time by re-entering the event loop.

But wait, there's a subtle issue here. When processing the queued message, `textParts`, `segmentStart`, `toolCount`, `turnStart`, `firstEventLogged`, `waitStart` are all reset. And new `StreamPreview` and `CompactProgressWriter` are created. This is all fine.

The issue I see is: the `session.AddHistory("user", queued.content)` is called at line 2831, which adds the queued message to session history. But the `processInteractiveEvents` also calls `session.AddHistory("assistant", ...)` for the response at line 2667. So history is correctly maintained.

Let me look at the `processInteractiveEvents` function signature and how it\'s called:\n\nIn `processInteractiveMessageWith` (line 2003):\n```go\ne.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey, msg.MessageID, turnStart, stopTyping, sendDone, msg.ReplyCtx)\n```\n\nAnd in `drainPendingMessages` (line 2970):\n```go\ne.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, sessionKey, "", time.Now(), stopTyping, sendDone, queued.replyCtx)\n```\n\nBoth call `processInteractiveEvents`. Now, inside `processInteractiveEvents`, at line 2775-2776:\n```go\nstate.mu.Lock()\nif len(state.pendingMessages) > 0 {\n```\n\nIt checks for pending messages and processes them. So if there are pending messages when entering `processInteractiveEvents`, the FIRST event loop iteration will process them. \n\nBut there\'s a subtlety: when `processInteractiveEvents` is called from `drainPendingMessages`, the `drainPendingMessages` function already dequeued one message and then calls `processInteractiveEvents`. Inside `processInteractiveEvents`, the loop checks for MORE pending messages (the remaining ones). If there are more, it processes them too. So the drain works recursively: `drainPendingMessages` handles one, `processInteractiveEvents` handles any additional ones.\n\nBut wait, what about the case where `processInteractiveMessageWith` calls `processInteractiveEvents` directly? At that point, `pendingMessages` is empty (no messages have been queued yet). During the loop, messages get queued. When EventResult arrives, the loop checks for queued messages and processes them one by one. This should work.\n\nOK let me try to think about this from a completely different angle. Let me look at the `drainOrphanedQueue` race more carefully.\n\nThe race guard is:\n```go\nif e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {\n    if session.TryLock() {\n        go e.drainOrphanedQueue(session, sessions, interactiveKey, agent, resolvedWorkspace)\n    }\n    return\n}\n```\n\nWhat if `queueMessageForBusySession` returns true (message queued), but then `session.TryLock()` also succeeds? This means the main processor has finished and unlocked. So `drainOrphanedQueue` is called.\n\nBut what if the main processor is in the middle of calling `drainPendingMessages` at the same time? Let\'s trace:\n\n1. Main processor: `processInteractiveEvents` returns (no queued messages in the loop).\n2. Main processor: about to call `drainPendingMessages`.\n3. Good, those tests pass. Let me now look more carefully at the `drainPendingMessages` and the `processInteractiveEvents` to see if there's an issue with how they interact.

Let me re-read the drain logic in the event loop (lines 2773-2848) more carefully. I want to check if there's a scenario where the `continue` loop would fail to pick up newly queued messages.

Actually, I just realized something critical. Let me look at the `processInteractiveEvents` function signature and how it's called:

In `processInteractiveMessageWith` (line 2003):
```go
e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey, msg.MessageID, turnStart, stopTyping, sendDone, msg.ReplyCtx)
```

And in `drainPendingMessages` (line 2970):
```go
e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, sessions, sessionKey, "", time.Now(), stopTyping, sendDone, queued.replyCtx)
```

Both call `processInteractiveEvents`. Now, inside `processInteractiveEvents`, at line 2775-2776:
```go
state.mu.Lock()
if len(state.pendingMessages) > 0 {
```

It checks for pending messages and processes them. So if there are pending messages when entering `processInteractiveEvents`, the FIRST event loop iteration will process them. 

But there's a subtlety: when `processInteractiveEvents` is called from `drainPendingMessages`, the `drainPendingMessages` function already dequeued one message and then calls `processInteractiveEvents`. Inside `processInteractiveEvents`, the loop checks for MORE pending messages (the remaining ones). If there are more, it processes them too. So the drain works recursively: `drainPendingMessages` handles one, `processInteractiveEvents` handles any additional ones.

But wait, what about the case where `processInteractiveMessageWith` calls `processInteractiveEvents` directly? At that point, `pendingMessages` is empty (no messages have been queued yet). During the loop, messages get queued. When EventResult arrives, the loop checks for queued messages and processes them one by one. This should work.

OK let me try to think about this from a completely different angle. Let me look at the `drainOrphanedQueue` race more carefully.

The race guard is:
```go
if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
    if session.TryLock() {
        go e.drainOrphanedQueue(session, sessions, interactiveKey, agent, resolvedWorkspace)
    }
    return
}
```

What if `queueMessageForBusySession` returns true (message queued), but then `session.TryLock()` also succeeds? This means the main processor has finished and unlocked. So `drainOrphanedQueue` is called.

But what if the main processor is in the middle of calling `drainPendingMessages` at the same time? Let's trace:

1. Main processor: `processInteractiveEvents` returns (no queued messages in the loop).
2. Main processor: about to call `drainPendingMessages`.
3. Handler: `session.TryLock()` fails (session still locked).
4. Handler: `queueMessageForBusySession` queues the message. Returns true.
5. Handler: `session.TryLock()` fails again (main processor hasn't unlocked yet). Returns.
6. Main processor: calls `drainPendingMessages(state, session, sessions, interactiveKey)`.
7. `drainPendingMessages` locks `state.mu`, finds the message, processes it. Good.

Now another scenario:
1. Main processor: `processInteractiveEvents` returns.
2. Main processor: calls `drainPendingMessages`.
3. `drainPendingMessages` locks `state.mu`, finds empty queue.
4. `drainPendingMessages` calls `session.Unlock()` and returns.
5. Handler: `session.TryLock()` - wait, the handler hasn't been called yet.
6. Handler is called. `session.TryLock()` fails (because `processInteractiveMessageWith` is about to unlock via defer? No, `unlocked = true` was set, so defer won't unlock).
7. Wait, let me re-read: `drainPendingMessages` returns true, which sets `unlocked = true` at line 2014. Then `processInteractiveMessageWith` returns. The defer sees `unlocked = true` and doesn't unlock.
8. So the session is now unlocked.
9. Later, handler is called for a new message. `session.TryLock()` succeeds. Normal processing.

But what if the handler is called BETWEEN step 4 and step 8? 
- Step 4: `drainPendingMessages` unlocks the session.
- Step 5-8: `processInteractiveMessageWith` returns.
- Between step 4 and step 8, could the handler be called? Yes, because the session is unlocked!

If the handler is called between step 4 and step 8:
- Handler: `session.TryLock()` succeeds (session was just unlocked).
- Handler: calls `processInteractiveMessageWith` in a new goroutine.
- The original `processInteractiveMessageWith` continues to return.

This is fine - the new message gets its own turn. But there's a subtle issue: what if between step 4 and the handler being called, another message was already queued by a DIFFERENT handler goroutine? Like this:

1. Main processor: `processInteractiveEvents` returns.
2. Handler A (for message 2): `session.TryLock()` fails, queues message 2.
3. Main processor: calls `drainPendingMessages`.
4. `drainPendingMessages` locks `state.mu`, finds message 2.
5. `drainPendingMessages` dequeues message 2, calls `processInteractiveEvents` for it.
6. Inside that `processInteractiveEvents`, while processing message 2...
7. Handler B (for message 3): `session.TryLock()` fails (still locked by drain), queues message 3.
8. Message 2's `processInteractiveEvents` finishes, returns.
9. `drainPendingMessages` loop: checks `state.pendingMessages`, finds message 3.
10. Processes message 3.
11. After message 3 is done, `drainPendingMessages` finds empty queue, unlocks.
12. Session is now unlocked. 

This seems to work. But wait, what if step 7 happens after step 9 checks and finds message 3, but before step 10 starts processing it? No, because step 7 requires `state.mu` (via `queueMessageForBusySession`), and step 9 holds `state.mu`. So the ordering is correct.

I really can't find the bug through code reading alone. Let me try to reproduce the issue with a test script.

Actually, let me look at the issue from the PR description one more time. The PR says this happens with feishu WebSocket mode. Let me check if there's something specific about how feishu dispatches messages that could cause this.

Looking at `dispatchMessage` in feishu.go (line 754): each message gets its own goroutine. The `downloadImage` call (line 795) takes time. So the sequence would be:

1. Image 1: goroutine starts, downloads image (takes ~500ms based on the log), calls handler.
2. Image 2: goroutine starts shortly after (maybe 200ms later based on log), downloads image, calls handler.
3. Image 3: goroutine starts shortly after, downloads image, calls handler.

But the key insight from the log is:
```
20:45:17.184 downloaded image  size=244513   ← 第1张
20:45:17.184 processing message              ← 第1张，进入处理

20:45:17.916 downloaded image  size=266337   ← 第2张
20:45:18.003 message received                ← 第3张消息到达
20:45:18.629 downloaded image  size=680155   ← 第3张
20:45:47.738 turn complete                   ← 第1张处理完毕
```

The first message starts processing at 17.184. Image 2's handler calls the engine at ~17.916. Image 3's handler calls the engine at ~18.629.

At 17.916, the session should be busy (locked at 17.184). So `queueMessageForBusySession` should queue it. At 18.629, same thing.

After turn completes at 47.738, the queue should be drained. But the log says no "processing queued message" appears after that.

So either:
1. `queueMessageForBusySession` is NOT being called (some other code path)
2. `queueMessageForBusySession` is called but returns false
3. The queue is working but the drain doesn't happen

Let me check if there's a code path where the message isn't queued. In `handleMessage`:

```go
if !session.TryLock() {
    trimmed := strings.TrimSpace(content)
    if isBtwCommand(trimmed) {
        // handle btw...
        return
    }
    // Session is busy — try to queue
    if e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg, interactiveKey) {
        // Race guard...
        return
    }
    e.reply(p, msg.ReplyCtx, e.i18n.T(MsgPreviousProcessing))
    return
}
```

So if `queueMessageForBusySession` returns false, it falls through to `MsgPreviousProcessing`. When would `queueMessageForBusySession` return false?

```go
func (e *Engine) queueMessageForBusySession(p Platform, msg *Message, interactiveKey string) bool {
    e.interactiveMu.Lock()
    state, hasState := e.interactiveStates[interactiveKey]
    e.interactiveMu.Unlock()

    if !hasState || state == nil || state.agentSession == nil || !state.agentSession.Alive() {
        return false
    }

    state.mu.Lock()
    if len(state.pendingMessages) >= maxQueuedMessages {
        state.mu.Unlock()
        return false
    }
    state.pendingMessages = append(state.pendingMessages, queuedMessage{...})
    ...
    return true
}
```

It returns false if:
1. No interactive state exists for the key (`!hasState`)
2. State is nil
3. `state.agentSession` is nil
4. `state.agentSession` is not alive
5. Queue is full (>= 5 messages)

Now, at 17.916 when message 2's handler runs, the interactive state should exist because message 1 already created it at 17.195. The agent session should be alive. The queue should not be full (it's the first queued message).

So `queueMessageForBusySession` should return true, and the message should be queued. Unless... there's a race condition where the state was cleaned up between message 1 starting and message 2 arriving?

Let me check if `cleanupInteractiveState` could be called. It's called from:
1. `processInteractiveEvents` at `channelClosed` (line 2879)
2. `processInteractiveEvents` at `EventError` with dead agent (line 2869)
3. `processInteractiveEvents` at idle timeout (line 2403)
4. `cleanupInteractiveState` directly from outside

None of these should happen during normal processing. So the state should be alive.

Hmm, wait. Let me look at this differently. What if the issue is with the `interactiveKey`? Let me check what `interactiveKey` is used.

```go
interactiveKey := msg.SessionKey
if e.multiWorkspace && wsSessions != nil {
    sessions = wsSessions
    agent = wsAgent

```

For non-multi-workspace mode (which is the typical case), `interactiveKey` is just `msg.SessionKey`. For feishu, this would be something like `feishu:chat_id:user_id`.

In `getOrCreateInteractiveStateWith`, the same `interactiveKey` is used to store the state. So it should match.

🎬 ACTION
cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/repro_test.go << 'EOF'
package core

import (
    "testing"
    "time"
)

func TestReproduceQueueIssue(t *testing.T) {
    p := &stubPlatformEngine{n: "test"}
    sess := newQueuingSession("qs-repro")
    agent := &controllableAgent{nextSession: sess}
    e := NewEngine("test", agent, []Platform{p}, "", LangEnglish)

    key := "feishu:chat123:user456"
    session := e.sessions.GetOrCreateActive(key)

    // Lock the session first (simulating first message being processed)
    if !session.TryLock() {
        t.Fatal("expected TryLock to succeed")
    }

    // Set up state with agent session - simulating an active turn
    state := &interactiveState{
        agentSession: sess,
        platform:     p,
        replyCtx:     "ctx",
    }
    e.interactiveMu.Lock()
    e.interactiveStates[key] = state
    e.interactiveMu.Unlock()

    // Now queue two messages (simulating rapid successive messages)
    msg2 := &Message{SessionKey: key, Content: "second message", ReplyCtx: "ctx2", Platform: "feishu", UserID: "user456"}
    ok2 := e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg2, key)
    if !ok2 {
        t.Fatal("expected msg2 to be queued")
    }

    msg3 := &Message{SessionKey: key, Content: "third message", ReplyCtx: "ctx3", Platform: "feishu", UserID: "user456"}
    ok3 := e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg3, key)
    if !ok3 {
        t.Fatal("expected msg3 to be queued")
    }

    // Verify both are in queue
    state.mu.Lock()
    qLen := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.mu.Unlock()
    if qLen != 2 {
        t.Fatalf("expected 2 queued messages, got %d", qLen)
    }

    // Now simulate the first turn completing
    // Send events for the initial turn
    session.AddHistory("user", "first message")

    // Start a goroutine that will push events for the queued messages
    // when Send is called
    go func() {
        // Turn 1 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response1", Done: true}
        // Wait for first queued message Send
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 1 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 2 result (for first queued message)
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response2", Done: true}
        // Wait for second queued message Send
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 2 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 3 result (for second queued message)
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response3", Done: true}
    }()

    sendDone := make(chan error, 1)
    sendDone <- nil  // initial send succeeded

    done := make(chan struct{})
    go func() {
        e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, e.sessions, key, "msg1", time.Now(), nil, sendDone, nil)
        close(done)
    }()

    select {
    case <-done:
    case <-time.After(5 * time.Second):
        t.Fatal("processInteractiveEvents did not complete")
    }

    // Verify queue is empty
    state.mu.Lock()
    remaining := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.mu.Unlock()
    if remaining != 0 {
        t.Fatalf("pendingMessages after processing = %d, want 0", remaining)
    }

    t.Log("SUCCESS: All queued messages were processed")
}
EOF
go test ./core/ -run TestReproduceQueueIssue -v -timeout 10s 2>&1

cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/repro_test.go << 'EOF'
package core

import (
    "testing"
    "time"
)

func TestReproduceQueueIssue(t *testing.T) {
    p := &stubPlatformEngine{n: "test"}
    sess := newQueuingSession("qs-repro")
    agent := &controllableAgent{nextSession: sess}
    e := NewEngine("test", agent, []Platform{p}, "", LangEnglish)

    key := "feishu:chat123:user456"
    session := e.sessions.GetOrCreateActive(key)

    // Lock the session first (simulating first message being processed)
    if !session.TryLock() {
        t.Fatal("expected TryLock to succeed")
    }

    // Set up state with agent session - simulating an active turn
    state := &interactiveState{
        agentSession: sess,
        platform:     p,
        replyCtx:     "ctx",
    }
    e.interactiveMu.Lock()
    e.interactiveStates[key] = state
    e.interactiveMu.Unlock()

    // Now queue two messages (simulating rapid successive messages)
    msg2 := &Message{SessionKey: key, Content: "second message", ReplyCtx: "ctx2", Platform: "feishu", UserID: "user456"}
    ok2 := e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg2, key)
    if !ok2 {
        t.Fatal("expected msg2 to be queued")
    }

    msg3 := &Message{SessionKey: key, Content: "third message", ReplyCtx: "ctx3", Platform: "feishu", UserID: "user456"}
    ok3 := e.queueMessageForBusySession(p, msg3, key)
    if !ok3 {
        t.Fatal("expected msg3 to be queued")
    }

    // Verify both are in queue
    state.mu.Lock()
    qLen := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.mu.Unlock()
    if qLen != 2 {
        t.Fatalf("expected 2 queued messages, got %d", qLen)
    }

    // Now simulate the first turn completing
    // Send events for the initial turn
    session.AddHistory("user", "first message")

    // Start a goroutine that will push events for the queued messages
    // when Send is called
    go func() {
        // Turn 1 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response1", Done: true}
        // Wait for first queued message Send
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 1 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 2 result (for first queued message)
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response2", Done: true}
        // Wait for second queued message Send
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 2 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 3 result (for second queued message)
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response3", Done: true}
    }()

    sendDone := make(chan error, 1)
    sendDone <- nil  // initial send succeeded

    done := make(chan struct{})
    go func() {
        e.processInteractiveEvents(state, session, e.sessions, key, "msg1", time.Now(), nil, sendDone, nil)
        close(done)
    }()

    select {
    case <-done:
    case <-time.After(5 * time.Second):
        t.Fatal("processInteractiveEvents did not complete")
    }

    // Verify queue is empty
    state.mu.Lock()
    remaining := len(state.pendingMessages)
    state.mu.Unlock()
    if remaining != 0 {
        t.Fatalf("pendingMessages after processing = %d, want 0", remaining)
    }

    t.Log("SUCCESS: All queued messages were processed")
}
EOF

cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/repro2_test.go << 'EOF'
package core

import (
    "testing"
    "time"
)

// TestReproduceFullFlow tests the complete flow from handleMessage through
// processInteractiveMessageWith to verify queued messages are processed.
func TestReproduceFullFlow(t *testing.T) {
    p := &stubPlatformEngine{n: "test"}
    sess := newQueuingSession("qs-full")
    agent := &controllableAgent{nextSession: sess}
    e := NewEngine("test", agent, []Platform{p}, "", LangEnglish)

    key := "feishu:chat123:user456"

    // Start first message processing in a goroutine
    msg1 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "first message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx1",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    }

    // Push events for turn 1 and subsequent queued message turns
    go func() {
        // Wait for Send to be called for turn 1
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 1 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 1 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response1", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 2 (first queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 2 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 2 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response2", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 3 (second queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 3 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 3 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response3", Done: true}
    }()

    // Send first message
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg1)

    // Wait a bit for the first message to start processing
    time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)

    // Now send second and third messages rapidly (simulating the PR scenario)
    msg2 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "second message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx2",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg2)

    msg3 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "third message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx3",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg3)

    // Wait for all processing to complete
    time.Sleep(3 * time.Second)

    // Check if messages were queued and processed
    session := e.sessions.GetOrCreateActive(key)
    history := session.GetHistory(100)

    var userMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "user" {
            userMsgs = append(userMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }

    t.Logf("User messages in history: %v", userMsgs)
    if len(userMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 user messages in history, got %d: %v", len(userMsgs), userMsgs)
    }

    var assistantMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "assistant" {
            assistantMsgs = append(assistantMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }
    t.Logf("Assistant messages in history: %v", assistantMsgs)
    if len(assistantMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 assistant messages in history, got %d: %v", len(assistantMsgs), assistantMsgs)
    }

    // Check sent messages
    sent := p.getSent()
    t.Logf("Platform sent messages: %v", sent)
    // We should have MsgMessageQueued replies for msg2 and msg3
    foundQueued := false
    for _, s := range sent {
        if s == "📬 Message received — will process after the current task finishes." {
            foundQueued = true
        }
    }
    if !foundQueued {
        t.Error("expected MsgMessageQueued reply for queued messages")
    }
}
EOF
cp /tmp/repro2_test.go /testbed/core/repro2_test.go
cd /testbed && go test ./core/ -run TestReproduceFullFlow -v -timeout 10s 2>&1

Interesting! The test passes. But I notice something interesting in the log output:

```
INFO processing message platform=feishu user=TestUser session=s1
...
INFO message received platform=feishu msg_id="" session=feishu:chat123:user456 user=TestUser content_len=13 has_images=false
INFO processing message platform=feishu user=TestUser session=s1
INFO message received platform=feishu msg_id="" session=feishu:chat123:user456 user=TestUser content_len=14 has_images=false
INFO message queued for busy session session=feishu:chat123:user456 user=TestUser queue_depth=1
```

Notice that msg3 was received and msg2 was "processing message" (not queued!). This is because msg2 arrived just as the first turn was completing but before it was locked. The timing is tricky.

But the important thing is that the queued message (msg3) WAS processed. The test passes.

Let me now test with images to see if there's any difference:







🎬 ACTION
cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/repro3_test.go << 'EOF'
package core

import (
    "testing"
    "time"
)

// TestReproduceWithImages tests the flow with image-only messages.
func TestReproduceWithImages(t *testing.T) {
    p := &stubPlatformEngine{n: "test"}
    sess := newQueuingSession("qs-img")
    agent := &controllableAgent{nextSession: sess}
    e := NewEngine("test", agent, []Platform{p}, "", LangEnglish)

    key := "feishu:chat123:user456"

    // Start first message processing in a goroutine - this one has an image
    msg1 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx1",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
        Images:     []ImageAttachment{{MimeType: "image/jpeg", Data: []byte("fake-image-data-1")}},
    }

    // Push events for turn 1 and subsequent queued message turns
    go func() {
        // Wait for Send to be called for turn 1
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 1 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 1 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response1", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 2 (first queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 2 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 2 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response2", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 3 (second queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 3 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        // Turn 3 result
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response3", Done: true}
    }()

    // Send first message
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg1)

    // Wait for it to start processing
    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)

    // Now send second and third messages rapidly with images
    msg2 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx2",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
        Images:     []ImageAttachment{{MimeType: "image/jpeg", Data: []byte("fake-image-data-2")}},
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg2)

    time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)

    msg3 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx3",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
        Images:     []ImageAttachment{{MimeType: "image/jpeg", Data: []byte("fake-image-data-3")}},
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg3)

    // Wait for all processing to complete
    time.Sleep(3 * time.Second)

    // Check history
    session := e.sessions.GetOrCreateActive(key)
    history := session.GetHistory(100)

    var userMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "user" {
            userMsgs = append(userMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }

    t.Logf("User messages in history: %v", userMsgs)
    if len(userMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 user messages in history, got %d: %v", len(userMsgs), userMsgs)
    }

    var assistantMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "assistant" {
            assistantMsgs = append(assistantMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }
    t.Logf("Assistant messages in history: %v", assistantMsgs)
    if len(assistantMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 assistant messages in history, got %d: %v", len(assistantMsgs), assistantMsgs)
    }

    // Check sent messages
    sent := p.getSent()
    t.Logf("Platform sent messages: %v", sent)
}
EOF

cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/repro4_test.go << 'EOF'
package core

import (
    "sync"
    "testing"
    "time"
)

// TestReproduceWithSlowTurn tests the flow when the first turn takes a long time.
func TestReproduceWithSlowTurn(t *testing.T) {
    p := &stubPlatformEngine{n: "test"}
    sess := newQueuingSession("qs-slow")
    agent := &controllableAgent{nextSession: sess}
    e := NewEngine("test", agent, []Platform{p}, "", LangEnglish)

    key := "feishu:chat123:user456"

    var turn1Started sync.WaitGroup
    turn1Started.Add(1)
    var allMessagesSent sync.WaitGroup
    allMessagesSent.Add(1)

    // Push events for turn 1 (delayed) and subsequent queued message turns
    go func() {
        // Wait for Send to be called for turn 1
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 1 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()

        turn1Started.Done() // Signal that turn 1 has started (session is busy)

        // Wait until all additional messages have been sent before completing turn 1
        allMessagesSent.Wait()

        // Now complete turn 1
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response1", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 2 (first queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 2 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response2", Done: true}

        // Wait for Send for turn 3 (second queued)
        sess.sendMu.Lock()
        for len(sess.sendCalls) < 3 {
            sess.sendMu.Unlock()
            time.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)
            sess.sendMu.Lock()
        }
        sess.sendMu.Unlock()
        sess.events <- Event{Type: EventResult, Content: "response3", Done: true}
    }()

    // Send first message
    go e.handleMessage(p, &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "first message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx1",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    })

    // Wait for turn 1 to start (session should be busy now)
    turn1Started.Wait()
    t.Log("Turn 1 started, session should be locked now")

    // Now send second and third messages rapidly while the session is busy
    msg2 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "second message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx2",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg2)

    time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)

    msg3 := &Message{
        SessionKey: key,
        Content:    "third message",
        ReplyCtx:   "ctx3",
        Platform:   "feishu",
        UserID:     "user456",
        UserName:   "TestUser",
    }
    go e.handleMessage(p, msg3)

    // Give them time to be processed
    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)

    // Signal that all messages have been sent
    allMessagesSent.Done()

    // Wait for all processing to complete
    time.Sleep(3 * time.Second)

    // Check history
    session := e.sessions.GetOrCreateActive(key)
    history := session.GetHistory(100)

    var userMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "user" {
            userMsgs = append(userMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }

    t.Logf("User messages in history: %v", userMsgs)
    if len(userMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 user messages in history, got %d: %v", len(userMsgs), userMsgs)
    }

    var assistantMsgs []string
    for _, h := range history {
        if h.Role == "assistant" {
            assistantMsgs = append(assistantMsgs, h.Content)
        }
    }
    t.Logf("Assistant messages in history: %v", assistantMsgs)
    if len(assistantMsgs) < 3 {
        t.Errorf("expected at least 3 assistant messages in history, got %d: %v", len(assistantMsgs), assistantMsgs)
    }

    // Check sent messages
    sent := p.getSent()
    t.Logf("Platform sent messages: %v", sent)

    // Verify queued messages were acknowledged
    foundQueueAck := false
    for _, s := range sent {
        if s == "📬 Message received — will process after the current task finishes." {
            foundQueueAck = true
        }
    }
    if !foundQueueAck {
        t.Error("expected MsgMessageQueued reply for queued messages")
    }
}
EOF
